content:
Ryan, that’s a fair and pastoral question.

A few anchors to keep in view:
- God is honored by faithfulness to what he actually said, not by saving face with culture. Sometimes following Jesus brings social cost (Matt 10:22; 1 Pet 4:14). But Scripture also calls us to avoid needless offense and to walk wisely toward outsiders (1 Cor 9:19–23; Col 4:5–6; 2 Tim 2:14, 24–25).
- Biblical inerrancy doesn’t mean we must force modern-science claims into ancient texts. Inerrancy means Scripture tells the truth in all it affirms, according to the authors’ intent and the literary form they used. The Bible often uses observational and poetic language—“sunrise,” “four corners of the earth,” “windows of heaven”—without intending a physics lesson.

About the “firmament”:
- The Hebrew raqia can mean “expanse.” In Genesis 1 birds fly “across the face of the raqia” (1:20), and the sun and moon are “set in the raqia” (1:17). Many inerrantists take this as the sky/space described in everyday terms, not a technical blueprint of cosmic architecture. Others hear ANE “dome” imagery as part of the packaging God used to communicate true theology about his ordering of creation. In both cases, the text is truthful in what it intends to teach: God made everything, ordered it, and rules it.
- The Bible is not embarrassed to speak phenomenologically. The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy explicitly acknowledges this. Augustine warned long ago not to tether the gospel to dubious claims about the natural world—lest we create unnecessary stumbling blocks.

So is God “big enough to be honored either way”? With core doctrines like the deity of Christ and the resurrection, no—we must not blur them. With secondary matters like how to parse raqia, faithful Christians can disagree and still honor God. Insisting that one narrow reading of “firmament” is the badge of faithfulness risks binding consciences where Scripture hasn’t.

What that means practically:
- Hold to the authority and truthfulness of Scripture. Read literally in the right sense: according to the author’s intent and the genre at hand.
- Don’t make a debated reading of Genesis 1 a test of orthodoxy. Keep the main thing the main thing: the gospel, the Creator’s goodness, human dignity as image-bearers, our accountability to God, and the hope secured in Christ.
- Be willing to bear reproach for Christ, but don’t pick fights you don’t need. If your conscience, study, and church tradition lead you to “expanse” rather than “solid dome,” say so without embarrassment. If you’re convinced of a dome reading, hold it humbly, acknowledge it’s debated, and don’t demand others adopt it to be “truly biblical.”
- With your kids and skeptical friends, model honest confidence: “Genesis gives true theology about who made the world and why. It uses the everyday sky-language ordinary people understand. I trust Scripture fully, and I’m not going to tie the gospel to a particular ancient cosmology debate.”

If it helps, good inerrantist-friendly guides on this include Vern Poythress (Redeeming Science) and John Frame (Doctrine of the Word of God). If you want to see a careful case for functional or temple-oriented readings of Genesis 1 that many conservatives interact with, John Walton is worth engaging—even if you don’t land everywhere he does.

Where are you personally landing right now, Ryan: are you feeling pressure to defend a dome, or pressure to abandon Scripture to avoid looking fringe? I’m glad to help you think it through with your context in view.
